body.notes: Participation in IETF meetings constitutes agreement to the Note Well, which permits recording, transcription, and public archival of proceedings. See also BCP 78 (RFC 5378) and BCP 79 (RFC 8179) for IPR policies.

transcript.text: It's [Music] Good morning everyone. Welcome to the IATF new participant program. Thank you all for coming along. Um I'm going to start off. Um I'll introduce myself in a minute but just so that we orient and make sure that you are all in the right place. This is the first session of today's new participant program. This is the introduction to the IETF and we will be talking about um about the IETF about participants about the structure of the IETF funding governance and policies and this is one of six presentations throughout the day and then we then follow up with a wrap-up session. Each of these presentations is given by somebody different um who will introduce themselves and um there is there will be time for questions and discussion within each of the sessions as well. So um my name is Jay Dailyaly. I am the IETF executive director. Um and I've been in this role for just over five years now. Um I've been in the internet for a long time. uh originally in networking and then in domain names. Um and you'll see when we discuss things later, we're a slightly unusual organization in that while I am the most um senior paid employee, I am not responsible for much of the things that you will be doing here, the work, the technology, um the development of standards because that is intentionally community-led. So um the the ITF is a complex organization. Um it's broad in scope. It covers variety of different activities. Um there are some detailed practices and detailed rules about how the ITF operates and we know this can be a high barrier of entry for new participants. And so we provide this whole day program to first of all teach you all the important things about the IETF to you to become productive and effective participants and also to reduce the chances of you giving up and running away um in horror. Um we also aim to build a cohort of new participants so that you get to meet the other people who are here who are starting in the IATF at the same time. um because you'll be working with them um if things go well for many years. And we also aim to give you a head start in meeting people. So we will introduce you to some of the people who have been here for a long time um throughout the week. We'll be doing that um so that you can begin to uh meet some of these people directly and learn about some of the hot topics. So that's our plan for this. Um so before we get into the substance of this we're just going to do a couple of short slides about um meter code. So this is um you've been sent details about this beforehand and I think many of you will have used it or have tested it. Um but we need to make sure that everybody is fully aware of this. This is the IETF custom tool for participation. Sorry, that should say participation, not remote participation. Um, it is what everybody needs to use in order to participate in the IATF because we aim to run a meeting that is um fully hybrid for people on site and people remote. And so we ask everybody on site to use meteo to um join the meeting queue and various other things to ensure that um you are uh that we are working aligned with those people from um working remotely. And so at each session there is a QR code here which we ask people to scan and then on their phones log in to meo um and you will have a simple um web-based client on your phone that doesn't do audio and video but lets you join in the chat and let you put your hand up to speak and things. So um and this is just a quick um explanation of how of the the features that are available in Mico. If you haven't looked at it yet, then um we very good eye to start looking at it now. Um so down the bottom as you can see we have the chat. Um we have a polling tool we call the show of hands tool. We have a map of the event. Um we have a link through to the shared notes because most sessions have shared notes that people write. We have the transcription and we have the list of participants. The transcription is a combination of human in some rooms and AI in other rooms and of variable quality as a result but um getting better all the time. So that's Mico. Um so now on to talk about the ITF then the the ITF is um a standards development organization at its heart um focus on internet technologies. So it is where people come to develop technology and agree technologies. But the basic premise for many people to come is that if you have a global standard then everybody can use it. If you don't have global standards then you can't communicate with other people or you can only communicate in a limited space and so that's why people come. Um it has no formal membership. Anyone can participate. you participate simply by participating. No permission is needed. Um people participate as individuals not representatives of their employer. Now what that means is that you somebody who comes as an individual has just as much say as somebody who comes from one of the world's largest companies. the companies cannot buy influence and cannot um have more of a say because they have more of a financial investment either in the internet or in the ITF. Um it is driven by market-based adoption. Uh a real standard is one that people use. A um many people regarded as a failure if the ITF produces a standard that nobody then implements because the work has then um not been useful. It is bottomup community controlled. So this is very important. We will see this throughout this. Everything you see about the structure of the IATF, the processes, the rules has been built by you in the community um and put together and all of the um work that I and the other people who work here do is to implement those processes that have been developed by the community volunteers. So it's completely controlled by the community. Um most decisions are made by rough consensus not voting. Now that will be explained later on in one of the later sessions. Um there is no formal role for governments. Now in this is in the same way there is no formal role for any organization. Um governments do send people who come and so we have plenty of government people. Um just as we have plenty of people from other um uh sectors which I will show you in a moment. Um but they come in the same way as uh everybody else comes. No special authority. And you may notice we have no sales going on. Um we don't ban marketing people. We just make it too complicated for them. Okay, that's the way. Right. So this is the mission of the IATF. It is not perhaps um going to make anybody jump up and clap, but it does certainly um encapsulate the IATF quite well. So it's to make the internet work better by producing highquality relevant technical documents that influence the way people design, use and manage the internet. And those three parts are useful important to remember design, use and manage because quite often participants only work in one of those areas and are sometimes not aware of the other areas of it as well. So the ITF was first formed in 19 um 86 um as an engineering activity. Uh the first meeting was attended by 21 US federal government funded researchers and some of them are still here. So you'll get to meet them and they've been to almost every meeting since. Um the internet standards process that is followed was first formally documented in 1992 and has been revised since and it initially met four times a year. now three and it's always been open to the public. Um the ITF is the home of the RSSE series but the RSSE series predates the IETF. RSC1 was published in 1969 by Steve Crocker of UCLA to organize the working notes of the ARPANET research program. Um and so many of you will know that some of the early um RSC's like um the some in the 800 series deal with email and other things. So it's it's been it's a longstanding um series and it's now approaching 10,000. So we're expecting RFC 10,000 um sometime next year I believe. Um and so online data access FTP was defined in one of the early RFC's and so the RFC series itself became the first online publication series because you could get it over FTP many years ago. Um and so for 28 years it was managed and edited by uh the internet pioneer John Postell and um it has matured significantly since but um you will see many of the orange origins of it still have some um impact today. So I'm now going to give you some idea about the other people that you'll be working with. Um this is useful because this is a big organization and needs a a degree of contextualization. So we have approximately 53,000 email addresses subscribed to our mailing lists. Um we have about 7,000 active participants per year. Now we measure that as the number of different email addresses that are used to send messages to our mailing lists, the number of individual different people that come to our meetings and the number of people who are listed as authors of our documents. And so we take the union of those three sets and that's how we end up with this number. Um so as you can see the um we ask people the first um chart here to tell us in this survey which we do once a year what kind of participant are you are you a regular participant as in that you read and post to email lists regularly and we have approximately 12% of people are in that category. Then we ask people are you an occasional one which means that they do post but not that often and we are at say 24%. Then we have people who read email lists but do not post to them and these are quite often people who are just reading one or two lists for specific um uh reasons and we have another 23% there. Then we know we have a group of people who monitor email lists for specific things such as a um specific piece of technology being developed, a specific change or something and they're using some form of um automated system or other things. And we have um almost 30% of people that and then um still we have plenty of previous participants who still see subscribe to the email list and then we have 6% of new participants. So you're in that group of new participants, approximately 6% of the um 7,000 active participants. Then we have the breakdown by region and as you can see Europe and North America dominate. Now the IATF rather unusually defines North America as USA and Canada. That's a historical thing. And um so uh and Mexico is in Latin America rather than North America. But that is a pretty straight um static breakdown of our um breakdown by region. So relatively low participation from the Middle East. Um and then when we look at the breakdown by gender as you can see this is very very heavily male oriented organization. We have um more and more um women joining and so I I don't know if you remember have a slide but the these are this is changing noticeably um amongst um new participants. Um we have a quite different breakdown there but this is our current breakdown. Um just to be clear, this is not something that many of us want to see as the way that it continues, but we think it's important to be transparent and um this level of transparency is something you will see throughout the IETF. So looking at employment, most people are employed. Um some are business owners, some are independent contractors and a small number retired and students and most people are in business. Um but then we have academia um coming in next and then the civil society and not for profofit after that. So we are still quite a broad um representation of people just heavily weighted towards people employed by industry. Um and then we asked people when they first participated. And so as you can see we have you know people right out in 1986. Um but and we have a relatively long I mean there are some ups and downs of curves here but we have people who do stay for a very long time and do continue to participate for a very long time. Um, some might say that's because it takes a long time to get things done, but generally this is because people find it potentially quite well they do find it very rewarding and um absorbing and do stay. It is not unusual for people who when they change employer to make it a condition of their employment that they continue to participate in the IETF. So the structure of the ITF this is really quite simple. Um so um we have a number of leadership bodies. Um and uh these are the leadership bodies across the top in different ways. Um the black is the ITF LLC which I work for. Um the yellow is the IESG. The ISG are actually the people in charge despite the way that this looks. Um we have um the IAB over in red and um I I'll talk a little bit more about these but underneath the ISG we have the areas down in yellow the working group chairs the working groups and the various parts of the teams and other things down the bottom. Um and under the IAB we have workshops and programs um that you'll see. And then many of you may be here for the IRTF um the research task force that's over on the far left and we have the research groups showing there. These other bits will become clearer over time. Um and um the this is effectively meant to show who appoints who and the one takeaway from this is that it is extremely difficult to capture the IETF because we have such a complex structure of appointments between people and things and almost all of this is volunteers and volunteer run. So as I said the internet engineering steering group the ISG um has overall responsibility for the standards process and technical management of the ITF. They're the people in charge. Um they are the area directors and the IATF chair Roman Dulu and so you will meet them um a number of them for at the um new participant sessions that we've got today and later in the week. the internet architecture board long-term technical direction for the internet architecture and it's responsible for external engagement both liaison and other engagement and uh it does a number of the key appointments and that's chaired by Tommy Pley then we have the IRSG which manages the internet research task force longer term research issues um chaired by um Durk Kush now um and they write RSC's the research groups but they don't produce standards um this is a a very much a research thing and they they work differently you know research does not have an end goal research is research um so it's a smaller part of things but an important part of the overall ITF the ITF administration LLC that I work for is the corporate and legal home um does uh we do finances contracted services. We run arrange the meetings. We do the legal side of things and it I have a board who are volunteers appointed through the nominating committee and um paid staff and um a number of um important contracts that we'll discuss. And as noticed, we have no influence over the technical content of internet standards. This is a separate organization that owns the intellectual property of the IETF to keep it safe and protect it from anything that might happen here.
